scenario: A Camunda 8.8 healthcare provider receives 12000 records per day at an inbound HTTP Webhook from a lab-results gateway. Payloads are shaped {"id": 1, "status": "OK"} and the webhook should only start a process when status equals "OK". The id field from each accepted payload must be exposed as the BPMN process variable myDocumentId, matching the example in the inbound connector docs.
question: Which combination of Activation condition and Result expression matches the documented HTTP webhook example?

options.text: Activation condition =(status = "OK") and Result expression ={myDocumentId: id} so the FEEL evaluates against the process variables of the running instance
Activation condition =(request.body.status = "OK") and Result expression ={myDocumentId: request.body.id} so the FEEL evaluates against the incoming request
Activation condition =(request.body.status = "OK") and Result variable myDocumentId only, without a Result expression, so the entire request body is bound to the variable
Activation condition =request.body.status and Result expression ={myDocumentId: request.body} so the status string itself is read as the boolean trigger value

a.text: Incorrect. The Activation condition cannot reference process variables; it only sees the inbound request payload, so referencing bare status without the request.body prefix would not evaluate the incoming field at all.

b.text: Correct. The documented Activation condition evaluates a FEEL boolean against the inbound payload via request.body.status, and the Result expression is a FEEL context that maps request.body.id onto the new process variable myDocumentId.

c.text: Incorrect. Result variable and Result expression are separate fields, and setting only Result variable binds the whole body, not the documented {myDocumentId: request.body.id} mapping shown in the webhook example.

d.text: Incorrect. The Activation condition expects a boolean FEEL expression, so returning the status string by itself is not equivalent to the documented equality comparison that gates trigger execution.

explanation: The inbound HTTP webhook example shows that Activation condition is a FEEL boolean evaluated against the inbound request and may not reference process variables, while Result expression is a FEEL context that maps request.body.* fields onto BPMN process variables of the new instance.

docs.title: Use inbound connectors - Example HTTP webhook
